summary: Kenneth reviews his weekly covered call results across 6 positions, netting $1,939 profit (3.2% return on $59.8k deployed). Key lesson: HIMS stock became too volatile for covered calls after CEO insider selling caused a 15% drop; he plans to switch to swing trading HIMS instead while continuing the wheel strategy on other positions.
takeaways: Exit covered call positions immediately when IV spikes dramatically—HIMS premiums jumped to $535/contract (from $317 two hours earlier) representing $2,100 vs $317 missed profit
CEO insider share sales typically signal liquidity needs (yachts, real estate) not company deterioration—stock usually recovers after the panic sell-off
Abandon covered calls on stocks with realized volatility >15% daily swings; redeploy capital to swing trading instead for better risk-adjusted returns
Track portfolio turnover: 6 positions rotated weekly, 2 assigned (Soundhound, D-Wave), 2 rolled (QuantumCape, Nphase), 2 unassigned (Oscar, HIMS)
Set strict position size: 400 shares = 4 covered call contracts per position to standardize premium collection
Accept sub-optimal exits ($317 on HIMS vs potential $2,100) as cost of not timing tops—this is unavoidable in options selling

strategy_candidates.change: Implement volatility-based filtering to pause covered call selling on individual stocks when daily moves exceed 10-12% or when IV rank jumps >30 points intraday; switch to swing trading or cash-secured puts instead
strategy_candidates.evidence: HIMS example: went from $58.55 to $65 (+11%) in hours, then crashed to $49.78 (-23% from peak). Kenneth explicitly stated 'I don't think I'm going to be selling covered calls on him stock anymore. It's just way way too volatile' and concluded 'I think a better move for me with HIMS is going to be to swing trade the stock, not sell covered calls.' This is a concrete rule for the Premium Desk to avoid capital lockup on volatile positions.

title: Part 19: $1939 profit in one week!
